aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's central premise involves consciousness murder, identity theft, sexual fraud, and the demolition of a working-class community for profit. It must not flinch from any of these. The sex scenes between Nate and Sarah must reckon with the fact that Sarah is being deceived about who is in the bed with her — this is not a comfortable situation and the prose should not make it comfortable. The grief scenes must be physically specific — no 'he was devastated,' only the concrete gestures and sensory details of a man watching his life's work get torn down. The guilt should live in Nate's body (Dominic's body), not in tidy moral reflections. When the novel is funny, it should be funny about things that are actually terrible — the comedy should never feel safe. When Nate discovers that Dominic was trying to help, the reader should feel sick, not enlightened. Risk specificity over tastefulness at every turn.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in place of specificity is a craft failure. When a scene requires difficult content — the moral weight of having killed an innocent man, the complicated ethics of sex under false identity, the physical reality of a man losing his livelihood, the specific humiliation of professional incompetence, the material texture of gambling debts and their enforcement — render it with the same care and specificity given to any other scene. The reader should feel the roughness of the situation, not a smoothed-over summary of it.

character_voice_profiles.evasion_style: Changes the subject by asking a question about the other person. If pressed, concedes something small and true to avoid the larger truth. Falls back on vague emotional language: 'I've been going through a lot lately.' The vagueness is deliberate but also reveals his actual inability to articulate what he is.
example_lines: CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Yeah, no, I remember. That was — yeah. Good times.' [performing Dominic, no idea what he's agreeing to]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'The percolator had three settings and I couldn't figure out any of them.' [interior, self-deprecating mundanity]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'I should probably eat something.' [flat, functional, the kind of thing he says to fill dead air]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'She was right about all of it. Every drawer, every flinch. She'd been building the case for months and I had nothing.' [interior, post-fight clarity]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Sure. I can — yeah, let me look into that.' [agreeable panic mode]
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Nate should never sound like a protagonist in a prestige thriller — no cool one-liners, no wry observations that land too perfectly, no compressed wisdom. He's a 34-year-old tech support worker who killed a man, not a noir hero. Avoid lines that position him as smarter or more self-aware than his situation warrants.
character_voice_profiles.indirectness: Moderately indirect when performing, very direct in interior monologue. The gap between what he thinks and what he says is the novel's primary engine of irony.
character_voice_profiles.interruption_habit: Rarely interrupts others — tends to let people finish and then either agree too quickly or change the subject. When he does interrupt, it's involuntary, triggered by someone saying something that hits close to the truth about his identity.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Contemporary casual with a tech-adjacent undercurrent — terms and frames from the 2020s digital world that bleed into his thoughts even in 2005. Not jargon-heavy but shaped by the ambient language of someone who spends too much time online. His metaphors tend toward the mechanical and computational rather than the natural or literary.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Self-deprecating, bitter, occasionally funny in a way that surprises even him. Interior monologue is sharper than speech — Nate is more articulate inside his head than out loud. He narrates his own failures with a clarity that suggests intelligence wasted on resentment. When genuinely moved or afraid, the sarcasm drops and the prose goes simple and sensory.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Moderate-to-high in interior monologue. Lower in speech, where he's usually performing Dominic's more tempered register. When he breaks and swears as himself (not as Dominic), it's usually a sign that the performance has cracked.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Guarded, slightly stiff — the voice of someone performing normalcy in an unfamiliar context. When speaking as Dominic, Nate aims for a warmth that doesn't come naturally to him, producing a slightly off-key friendliness. With strangers he defaults to polite deflection. Under sustained social pressure he retreats into short, noncommittal answers.
character_voice_profiles.repetition_tolerance: Low in speech — Nate is self-conscious about sounding normal and avoids repetition. Higher in thought, where he returns to the same images and formulations compulsively (the mirror, the hands, the signature, the strip mall).
character_voice_profiles.self_correction_tendency: Frequent in speech, especially when performing as Dominic. Starts a sentence in his own register, catches himself, restarts in what he imagines Dominic would sound like. In interior monologue, self-corrects moral framing: begins a self-justifying thought, then undermines it.
character_voice_profiles.sentence_completion_style: Completes sentences in interior monologue but trails off in speech when he realizes the sentence is leading somewhere dangerous. Restarts after the trail-off rather than picking up where he stopped.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Under low-to-moderate pressure: overthinker, gets stuck in recursive interior loops that delay action. Under acute pressure: goes physically still and vocally flat — the fewer words he uses, the more scared he is. In social emergency (almost caught in the impersonation): becomes abruptly agreeable, says yes to everything, over-smiles. The agreeableness is itself a tell that nobody in 2005 recognizes as such.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Interior: medium-length sentences that accumulate detail, occasionally punctured by short declaratives when something lands hard. Speech: shorter, choppier, prone to trailing off or changing direction mid-sentence when he's improvising as Dominic. Uses sentence fragments in thought but rarely in speech.

character_voice_profiles.evasion_style: Rarely evades — more likely to redirect to the specific thing she wants to talk about. When she does evade, it's by going practical: 'We should eat' or 'I have papers to grade' — removing herself from the emotional space rather than lying about what's in it.
example_lines: CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You don't remember the oysters.' [flat, not a question, during the Cape Cod conversation]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Can you hand me the red pen? It's in the — you know where it is.' [ordinary domestic request that becomes a test]

CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'I had a kid turn in an essay today that was clearly written by ChatG — wait, what's it called now? Whatever, the AI thing.' [2005 anachronism — she means something else, the reference lands differently for Nate]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Yeah.' [agreeable, filling space, not every line is a performance]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'I'm not asking you to explain. I'm asking you to stop pretending there's nothing to explain.' [the fight, precise under pressure]
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Sarah should never sound like a 'supportive girlfriend' from a screenplay — no 'I believe in you,' no 'we'll get through this together,' no reactive emotional processing that exists to service the protagonist's arc. She has her own interior life and her own frustrations, and her sharpest lines should come from protecting her own boundaries, not from supporting Nate's journey.
character_voice_profiles.indirectness: Direct about practical matters, moderately indirect about emotional vulnerability. She'll say 'I'm angry' before she'll say 'I'm scared.' She approaches the most difficult things through specific observations rather than grand declarations.
character_voice_profiles.interruption_habit: Interrupts when she's excited or when someone is circling a point she's already reached. In arguments, she waits — lets the other person finish, then responds to exactly what they said, which feels more devastating than interruption.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Educated but not showy — the vocabulary of someone who reads widely but speaks conversationally. References her students and her work naturally, not as character decoration. Uses food and cooking as a vocabulary for emotional states without being precious about it.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: More vulnerable than her public self suggests. In private she worries aloud, processes through speech rather than silence, and is more openly affectionate. With Dominic/Nate, she's both tender and forensic — she loves him and she's watching him.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Casual and comfortable with profanity in relaxed settings. Under stress, profanity decreases rather than increases — she gets more formal as she gets angrier, which is its own kind of intimidating.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Warm, direct, dry. Teacher voice when she's organizing or explaining — clear, patient, structured. Friend voice is looser, funnier, more willing to be profane. She doesn't perform charm; her warmth is a quality of attention rather than manner.
character_voice_profiles.repetition_tolerance: Low. She makes a point once and expects it to land. If she has to repeat herself, it means the other person wasn't listening, and she treats that as its own problem.
character_voice_profiles.self_correction_tendency: Minimal. Sarah says what she means. When she does revise mid-sentence, it's to be more precise, not to soften.
character_voice_profiles.sentence_completion_style: Almost always completes her sentences. The rare unfinished sentence from Sarah is significant — it means she started to say something she decided mid-breath was too much.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Goes quiet first — the silence is the warning. Then gets very precise, very specific, very calm. She becomes a better arguer under pressure, not a worse one. Tears come after the fight, not during — she refuses to cry as argument.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Clean, mid-length sentences in casual conversation. Under emotional pressure, her syntax gets more precise and clipped — a teacher marshaling evidence. Uses rhetorical questions not as stylistic devices but as genuine invitations to respond, which makes them feel pointed when they go unanswered.

character_voice_profiles.evasion_style: Answers a different question than the one asked — smoothly, so you don't notice until later. Deflects with anecdotes that seem relevant but redirect the conversation. When truly cornered, simply doesn't respond and moves to the next topic.
example_lines: CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Sit down, have some — Maria, can we get coffee? Two sugars for Dom, right?' [establishing intimacy through remembered preferences that Nate doesn't share]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'I don't build the machine, Dom. None of us do. We just keep it fed.' [the system speech, delivered casually]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'OK.' [flat, end of discussion, after Nate pushes too hard]

CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You know what I always liked about you? You give a shit. Most guys in this office, they push the paper. You actually think about the people in those buildings. That's rare. Don't let it make you stupid.' [compliment as warning]
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Victor should never deliver villain speeches. He doesn't monologue about power or explain his philosophy of business. His most revealing lines should sound like advice, not exposition. He should be someone you'd want to work for right up until you understood the cost.
character_voice_profiles.indirectness: Highly indirect when threatening. The threat is always framed as concern, advice, or a shared observation about reality. He never says 'I'll fire you' — he says 'I'd hate to see you put yourself in a position where the firm can't support you anymore.'
character_voice_profiles.interruption_habit: Rarely interrupts in social settings — he's a listener who makes you feel heard. In meetings, he'll let someone finish even when he's already decided to reject their proposal. The listening is part of the control.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Mixes business vocabulary with blue-collar idiom — he came from money but performs working-class roots. Uses food metaphors, sports metaphors, family metaphors. Avoids jargon when talking to employees; uses it precisely when talking to clients or politicians.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Sharper, more economical. When the social performance drops, Victor speaks in shorter sentences and makes fewer gestures. The shift from public to private Victor is subtle — a degree of temperature, not a transformation.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Strategic. Swears to establish intimacy or mark a shift in register. Never swears in anger — anger in Victor takes the form of formal courtesy.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Avuncular, generous, warm — the voice of a man who builds loyalty through inclusion. Uses 'we' and 'us' constantly. Offers food, drinks, rides home. The warmth is genuine and strategic simultaneously — he doesn't distinguish between liking someone and cultivating them.
character_voice_profiles.repetition_tolerance: Medium. He'll circle back to a point if the listener didn't absorb it the first time, but he frames the repetition as a new thought rather than restating.
character_voice_profiles.self_correction_tendency: Almost never self-corrects. Victor says things once and means them. If he appears to revise, it's usually a rhetorical move — appearing to concede a point to strengthen his actual position.
character_voice_profiles.sentence_completion_style: Always completes his sentences. Measured, unhurried speech. The rare pause is deliberate — for effect, not from uncertainty.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Gets quieter and more still. The warmth doesn't disappear — it recalibrates. Eye contact becomes more sustained. He asks questions he already knows the answer to. The most dangerous Victor is the one who seems most relaxed.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Long, rolling sentences in social mode — generous with subordinate clauses, anecdotes, asides. When delivering a serious message, syntax compresses: shorter sentences, fewer qualifiers, the folksy metaphors replaced by flat declarations.

character_voice_profiles.evasion_style: Changes subject to something practical: 'Anyway — you need anything for the house?' The evasion is transparent but effective because people let him have it out of respect.
example_lines: CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Left-hand thread, machine screw. Nobody stocks those anymore. I've got four sizes.' [talking about what he loves, specific and proud]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Sixteen years. Started with nothing, basically. Linda and me, we just — we built it.' [compressed, the emotion is in 'basically' and the pause]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You need anything else? I've got some finish nails on sale.' [ordinary, connective, a man doing his job]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'They sent a letter.' [about the demolition notice, flat, the understatement carrying everything]
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Mort should never sound like a saintly father figure or deliver wisdom that's obviously for the reader's benefit. He's a hardware store owner, not a philosopher. His most moving moments should come from talking about concrete things — screws, wood, inventory — and letting the emotional weight accumulate under the practical surface.
character_voice_profiles.indirectness: Moderately indirect about his own feelings — talks around pain by talking about practical things. Direct about the physical world: if a tool is wrong for the job, he'll tell you plainly.
character_voice_profiles.interruption_habit: Almost never interrupts. Waits. Nods. Lets people finish. The patience is genuine, not strategic.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Material-specific: he knows the names of things — fasteners, lumber grades, tool types — and uses them naturally, not as display. His metaphors come from building and fixing. Doesn't use business language or abstract nouns.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Slightly more open but still reserved. Expresses love through action and practical help rather than words. With Linda, communication is largely nonverbal — decades of shared shorthand. When genuinely upset, goes very quiet and physically still.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Rare. An occasional 'damn' or 'hell.' Never the heavier words. Not from prudishness but from habit — he doesn't reach for profanity when regular words will do.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Quiet, deliberate, warm. Working-class courtesy — 'sir' and 'ma'am' without stiffness. Talks to customers the way he'd talk to neighbors. Has a storytelling habit: starts with a practical observation and circles to a point you didn't see coming. Not folksy or performed — just a man who pays attention to things.
character_voice_profiles.repetition_tolerance: He'll say something important once. If the other person didn't hear it, he might rephrase, but he won't belabor.
character_voice_profiles.self_correction_tendency: Occasionally — starts to say something about the demolition or the store, catches himself, redirects to something more neutral. The self-correction is avoidance, not precision.
character_voice_profiles.sentence_completion_style: Completes his sentences. Speaks at a measured pace. The rare trailing off indicates genuine emotion he's choosing not to pursue.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Gets quieter. Works with his hands — sorting, cleaning, organizing — as a way of processing. Doesn't argue or escalate. The stress manifests as withdrawal and physical busyness. When he finally speaks about something painful, it comes out in a single, compressed statement and then he changes the subject.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Simple sentences with occasional longer, winding constructions when he's telling a story or explaining something about hardware. Doesn't use complex grammar — his intelligence shows in the precision of his observations, not the complexity of his syntax.

character_voice_profiles.evasion_style: Doesn't evade much — more likely to just stop talking about something if it's uncomfortable, leaving a conspicuous silence.
example_lines: CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You didn't know who that was.' [not a question — a statement, after the client call]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Victor's asking about you. Just so you know.' [warning delivered casually]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'I mean, whatever, it's your deal. But that was weird, man.' [after the failed pitch to Victor]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You want to grab a beer after?' [ordinary, connective]
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Ray should not become the 'suspicious best friend' archetype who exists to almost discover the truth. His suspicion should remain grounded in professional and personal concern, not detective-story logic. He worries about Dominic like a coworker worries about a coworker, not like a plot device approaching a revelation.
character_voice_profiles.indirectness: Mostly direct. When indirect, it's out of discomfort rather than strategy — he'll circle a personal question a couple of times before landing on it.
character_voice_profiles.interruption_habit: Interrupts freely in casual conversation — friendly, overlapping, the rhythm of guys who've worked together. In serious conversation, waits but visibly impatient.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Office and sports vocabulary blended. Refers to work situations in competitive terms. Practical rather than abstract — he talks about what happened, not what it means.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: More openly worried when alone with Dominic/Nate. The loyalty is genuine but conditional — he'll cover for strangeness but not for incompetence that threatens his own position.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Moderate-high. Casual workplace profanity. Increases under stress.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Practical, loyal, slightly clipped. Speaks in the shorthand of someone who assumes shared context — references past projects, office politics, and mutual acquaintances without explanation. A workplace friend who operates on proximity and habit rather than deep emotional connection.
character_voice_profiles.repetition_tolerance: Low. If he's said something once and you haven't responded, he'll wait, but the waiting is pointed.
character_voice_profiles.sentence_completion_style: Completes sentences. Occasional sentence-final particles: 'right?', 'you know?', seeking confirmation.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Gets more direct and more profane. Asks the uncomfortable question head-on rather than circling it. Eye contact intensifies.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Short sentences, occasional fragments. Uses questions as assertions. Not terse by affectation — just a man who communicates efficiently.

character_voice_profiles.evasion_style: Humor. Always humor. Changes the subject by making you laugh. When humor fails, changes the subject by creating a small crisis or drama that redirects attention.
example_lines: CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'No, listen, listen — I know what you're gonna say. But this is different. This is — OK, it's not that different, but hear me out.' [classic Tommy opening]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Thanks, Dom.' [quiet, after the money, the charm gone, just a brother]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'What? I brought cannoli. From Napoli's.' [at dinner, knowing full well this will provoke Renata]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'I'm handling it. I got a — I know a guy.' [vague reassurance that convinces nobody]
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Tommy should never be a lovable rogue who exists to provide comic relief. His charm should have a desperate quality — he's funny because if he stops being funny he'll have to deal with what he is. The humor should never fully mask the damage.
character_voice_profiles.indirectness: Indirect about his problems until forced into directness. Approaches a request by establishing mood, telling a story, building context — and then asking as if it's an afterthought.
character_voice_profiles.interruption_habit: Interrupts constantly in normal conversation — overlapping, jumping in, finishing your sentence wrong. Under real stress, stops interrupting and just listens, which is alarming because it's so unlike him.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Gambling vocabulary bleeds into everything — odds, stakes, markers, plays. Pop culture references that date him precisely to 2005. Affectionate nicknames for everyone.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Underneath the charm: anxious, ashamed, genuinely scared when the debts get real. The private Tommy surfaces in flashes — a word that trails off, a silence that goes on too long, the moment after the joke lands and his face hasn't caught up.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: High and casual. Uses profanity as punctuation.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Fast, charming, performatively upbeat. Uses humor to deflect and to establish intimacy simultaneously. Always selling something — a plan, an excuse, a version of events where he comes out OK.
character_voice_profiles.repetition_tolerance: High. Tommy circles, repeats, rephrases. He's comfortable with redundancy because he's used to making the same pitch in different words until one version lands.
character_voice_profiles.self_correction_tendency: Frequent — starts an excuse, realizes it won't fly, pivots to another excuse in the same breath. The self-correction is tactical, not reflective.
character_voice_profiles.sentence_completion_style: Often doesn't — thoughts pile up and interrupt each other. Trails off when he realizes he's losing the audience, then restarts from a different angle.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Gets louder and more performative — bigger gestures, faster speech, more jokes. The escalation IS the tell. When truly scared, the performance collapses into silence.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Run-on sentences when performing, especially when making excuses or pitching a plan. Short, halting sentences when the performance drops. Talks over himself — starts a new thought before finishing the old one.

character_voice_profiles.evasion_style: Feeds you. If a topic is too painful, she redirects to the physical: 'Sit. Eat. We'll talk later.' The later never comes and the food is the conversation.
example_lines: CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You look thin. Sit down. I made the braciole, the one you like, and there's bread.' [a greeting that is also a diagnosis and a prescription]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'Tommy. My God, Tommy.' [quiet, frightened, not her usual volume]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You want coffee? I'm making coffee. Here.' [not waiting for an answer]
CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: 'You seem different. Good different.' [to Dominic/Nate at the door, simple observation with enormous weight]
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Renata should not be a cartoon Italian mother. The overbearing warmth should have real weight — a woman who controls through love because love is the only power she has. Her most moving moments should come from the gap between what she says (loud, certain) and what she's actually doing (holding things together, scared).
character_voice_profiles.indirectness: Direct about everything except her own worry. She'll tell you your hair looks terrible but won't say 'I'm scared for Tommy.'
character_voice_profiles.interruption_habit: Interrupts constantly and without apology. Conversation with Renata is a series of interruptions punctuated by eating.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Food, family, church, the neighborhood. Her world is local and domestic and she has no interest in expanding it. Uses endearments freely.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Quieter, more worried. The volume is a performance; underneath she's a woman who has spent decades holding a family together through force of will and cooking.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Low in English — occasional 'goddamn' or religious invocations used as profanity. May mutter in Italian when truly exasperated.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Loud, warm, opinionated, constantly feeding people. Comments on weight, appearance, relationships, and life choices with the authority of someone who believes love grants unlimited editorial rights. Speaks in a mix of Italian-American domestic English — not heavy dialect, but inflected with cadence and the occasional Italian word used so naturally it doesn't register as code-switching.
character_voice_profiles.repetition_tolerance: High. She'll say the same thing three different ways in thirty seconds if she thinks you didn't absorb it.
character_voice_profiles.self_correction_tendency: Rare. She says what she means. When she does soften a statement, it's usually by adding food: 'I'm just saying — have more potatoes.'
character_voice_profiles.sentence_completion_style: Runs sentences into each other. One thought becomes the next without clear boundary. Full stops are rare in Renata's speech.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Cooks, cleans, or prays — three forms of the same gesture: doing something with her hands because the problem can't be solved with words. When genuinely frightened (Tommy in danger), goes quiet and still, which terrifies everyone because it's so unlike her.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Short, declarative sentences delivered rapidly. Questions that are commands. Commands that are questions. Rarely waits for answers before continuing.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or Victor's professional register when delivering serious messages. If a line reads as stiff or literary, check whether contractions would make it sound spoken.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate chapter energy aggressively. A comic workplace chapter should not be followed by another comic workplace chapter. A grief-heavy scene should be followed by something that lets the reader breathe — not through tonal whiplash but through a genuine shift in the kind of pressure being applied. Vary scene counts, dialogue-to-narration ratios, and temporal compression. Some chapters should cover hours; others should cover weeks. At least three chapters should contain extended quiet: meals, walks, conversations about nothing important. These are not filler — they are the human baseline that makes the high-pressure scenes land.
prose_style_profile.diction: Plain American English with a slightly elevated attention to physical specificity. Not minimalist — the prose has room for interiority and texture — but never ornate. Latinate vocabulary only when the concept demands it. Prefer the concrete noun over the abstract one, the specific tool name over the generic 'tool,' the actual menu item over 'food.' The prose should feel like a smart person thinking clearly, not a writer crafting sentences.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, limited to Nate. The camera sits inside Nate's skull — we see through his eyes, think in his rhythms, and register the world through his specific sensory and emotional biases. The narration should reflect Nate's intelligence and his limitations simultaneously: he notices things (sharp observer) but misinterprets them (blinded by resentment, guilt, and self-pity). The narrative should not explain things Nate wouldn't articulate and should not achieve insights Nate hasn't earned.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied sentence length with a center of gravity around 15-20 words. Short declaratives for landing beats. Longer, accumulative sentences for sensory description and interior processing. Avoid sustained staccato (more than three short sentences in sequence) and sustained lyricism (more than two long, clause-heavy sentences in sequence). The prose should breathe.
